is the female representative in international [women's football](/wiki/Women%27s_association_football "Women's association football") for [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). The team was formed in 2010 and has not yet qualified for the [AFC Women's Asian Cup](/wiki/AFC_Women%27s_Asian_Cup "AFC Women's Asian Cup") or the [FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "FIFA Women's World Cup"), but has competed in five editions of the biennial [SAFF Women's Championship](/wiki/SAFF_Women%27s_Championship "SAFF Women's Championship").

The **Pakistan women's national football team** is the female representative in international [women's football](/wiki/Women%27s_association_football "Women's association football") for [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). The team was formed in 2010 and has not yet qualified for the [AFC Women's Asian Cup](/wiki/AFC_Women%27s_Asian_Cup "AFC Women's Asian Cup") or the [FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "FIFA Women's World Cup"), but has competed in five editions of the biennial [SAFF Women's Championship](/wiki/SAFF_Women%27s_Championship "SAFF Women's Championship").

Its under-19 and under-16 teams have competed in the qualification rounds of the [2019 AFC U-19 Women's Championship](/wiki/2019_AFC_U-19_Women%27s_Championship_qualification "2019 AFC U-19 Women's Championship qualification") and [2019 AFC U-16 Women's Championship](/wiki/2019_AFC_U-16_Women%27s_Championship_qualification "2019 AFC U-16 Women's Championship qualification"), respectively.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pakistan_women%27s_national_football_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Beginnings (2010–2011)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pakistan_women%27s_national_football_team&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Beginnings (2010–2011)")]

The Pakistan women's national football team made its international debut at the [11th South Asian Games](/wiki/Football_at_the_2010_South_Asian_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_tournament "Football at the 2010 South Asian Games – Women's tournament") in [Dhaka](/wiki/Dhaka "Dhaka"). [Midfielder](/wiki/Midfielder "Midfielder") [Ishrat Fatima](/w/index.php?title=Ishrat_Fatima_(footballer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ishrat Fatima (footballer) (page does not exist)") of [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore")'s Sports Sciences Department ([Punjab University](/wiki/Punjab_University,_Lahore "Punjab University, Lahore")) was the first captain of the team. The team played their first match on 31 January 2010 against [India](/wiki/India_women%27s_national_football_team "India women's national football team") at the [Bangabandhu National Stadium](/wiki/Bangabandhu_National_Stadium "Bangabandhu National Stadium").[[3]](#cite_note-3) It also faced off against [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh_women%27s_national_football_team "Bangladesh women's national football team"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka_women%27s_national_football_team "Sri Lanka women's national football team"), and [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal_women%27s_national_football_team "Nepal women's national football team") in that tournament, winning against Sri Lanka via [walkover](/wiki/Walkover "Walkover")[[4]](#cite_note-4) while losing the other two fixtures. It came in 4th out of 5 teams.

In November 2010, [Tariq Lutfi](/wiki/Tariq_Lutfi "Tariq Lutfi") was appointed as the coach of the team.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) In December 2010, the team, under Lutfi and the new captain, Sana Mehmood ([Young Rising Star](/wiki/Young_Rising_Stars_F.F.C. "Young Rising Stars F.F.C.")'s captain and [central defender](/wiki/Central_defender "Central defender")), participated in the [inaugural SAFF Women's Championship](/wiki/2010_SAFF_Women%27s_Championship "2010 SAFF Women's Championship") held at [Cox's Bazar Stadium](/wiki/Cox%27s_Bazar_Stadium "Cox's Bazar Stadium"). It stood second Group B, winning against [the Maldives](/wiki/Maldives_women%27s_national_football_team "Maldives women's national football team") (2–1) and [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan_women%27s_national_football_team "Afghanistan women's national football team") (3–0), while losing heavily to [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal_women%27s_national_football_team "Nepal women's national football team") (0–12). Pakistan lost 8–0 against [India](/wiki/India_women%27s_national_football_team "India women's national football team") in the semifinal and were thus eliminated. In this tournament, [Mehwish Khan](/wiki/Mehwish_Khan "Mehwish Khan") became the first ever goal-scorer for Pakistan (in the match against Maldives).

As a result of these official matches, Pakistan entered the [FIFA Women's World Rankings](/wiki/FIFA_Women%27s_World_Rankings "FIFA Women's World Rankings") for the first time on 18 March 2011, placing 121st in the World and 22nd in Asia.

### Further participations (2011–2014)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pakistan_women%27s_national_football_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Further participations (2011–2014)")]

In May 2011, four members of the team ([Abiha Haidar](/wiki/Abiha_Haider "Abiha Haider"), Roshnan Ali, Sara Mumtaz and coach [Ishrat Fatima](/w/index.php?title=Ishrat_Fatima_(footballer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ishrat Fatima (footballer) (page does not exist)")) went to the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") for a two-week FIFA Women's World Cup Developing Program. They attended seminars, programs, meetings, and practice sessions in [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), and [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), and visited various local colleges and universities. The group was also hosted for a special dinner at the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") by the US Government for boosting bilateral relations through sports promotion and development.

In September 2012, the team participated in the [2nd SAFF Women's Championship](/wiki/2012_SAFF_Women%27s_Championship "2012 SAFF Women's Championship") held in [Colombo](/wiki/Colombo "Colombo"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"). Pakistan lost against Nepal (0–8) and Afghanistan (0–4) before earning a consolation win against the Maldives (3–0).

Pakistan played its first international friendly series against [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain_women%27s_national_football_team "Bahrain women's national football team") in October 2014.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Three matches were played at the [Bahrain National Stadium](/wiki/Bahrain_National_Stadium "Bahrain National Stadium") in [Riffa](/wiki/Riffa "Riffa"), with the home side winning all of them. These matches were organized to help prepare the team for the upcoming 3rd edition of the [SAFF Women's Championship](/wiki/SAFF_Women%27s_Championship "SAFF Women's Championship").

Pakistan hosted the [2014 SAFF Women's Championship](/wiki/2014_SAFF_Women%27s_Championship "2014 SAFF Women's Championship") in November 2014, with all matches being held at [Jinnah Sports Stadium](/wiki/Jinnah_Sports_Stadium "Jinnah Sports Stadium") in [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad"). The hosts lost against [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka_women%27s_national_football_team "Sri Lanka women's national football team") (1–2)[[7]](#cite_note-7) and [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal_women%27s_national_football_team "Nepal women's national football team") (0–2), but won against [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan_women%27s_national_football_team "Bhutan women's national football team") (4–1),[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8) scoring four goals in a match for the first time.

### Hiatus (2014–2022)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pakistan_women%27s_national_football_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Hiatus (2014–2022)")]

Main article: [Crisis at Pakistan Football Federation](/wiki/Pakistan_Football_Federation#Crisis "Pakistan Football Federation")

From November 2014 to September 2022, the team saw no action due to the [Pakistan Football Federation](/wiki/Pakistan_Football_Federation "Pakistan Football Federation") (PFF) being suspended several times by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") for infighting and third-party interference.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The suspensions were removed occasionally, but not in time for Pakistan to participate in the [2016](/wiki/2016_SAFF_Women%27s_Championship "2016 SAFF Women's Championship") and [2019](/wiki/2019_SAFF_Women%27s_Championship "2019 SAFF Women's Championship") editions of the SAFF Women's Championship, and in the [2016](/wiki/Football_at_the_2016_South_Asian_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_tournament "Football at the 2016 South Asian Games – Women's tournament") and [2019](/wiki/Football_at_the_2019_South_Asian_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_tournament "Football at the 2019 South Asian Games – Women's tournament") editions of the [South Asian Games](/wiki/Football_at_the_South_Asian_Games "Football at the South Asian Games"). As a result of no matches being played, the team lost its FIFA ranking.

### Return (2022–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pakistan_women%27s_national_football_team&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Return (2022–present)")]

On 30 June 2022, FIFA lifted PFF's suspension after a period of 14 months.[[11]](#cite_note-11) As a result, PFF was able to send in Pakistan women's team's entry for the [2022 SAFF Women's Championship](/wiki/2022_SAFF_Women%27s_Championship "2022 SAFF Women's Championship"). The entry was confirmed on 9 July 2022, which meant that the team would be playing its first international match since 2014.[[12]](#cite_note-12) A 35-member training camp was held at Lahore in August 2022 under new head coach [Adeel Rizki](/wiki/Adeel_Rizki "Adeel Rizki"),[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13) after which a 23-member squad was announced on 24 August 2022. [Hajra Khan](/wiki/Hajra_Khan_(footballer) "Hajra Khan (footballer)"), [Malika-e-Noor](/wiki/Malika-e-Noor "Malika-e-Noor"), [Syeda Mahpara](/wiki/Syeda_Mahpara "Syeda Mahpara"), Nisha Ashraf, Roshnan Ali, [Sahar Zaman](/wiki/Sahar_Zaman_(footballer) "Sahar Zaman (footballer)"), and Zulfia Nazir were the only players who were part of the team that last played in 2014; the rest were given their first call-ups. Two overseas players, [Nadia Khan](/wiki/Nadia_Khan_(footballer) "Nadia Khan (footballer)") and [Maria Khan](/wiki/Maria_Khan "Maria Khan"), were also included for the first time.[[14]](#cite_note-:1-14) The captaincy was handed over to Maria Khan from Hajra Khan,[[15]](#cite_note-15) while Malika-e-Noor was named the vice-captain.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Pakistan made its return to international football on 7 September 2022, with 58th-ranked India being its first opponent in the group stage of the [2022 SAFF Women's Championship](/wiki/2022_SAFF_Women%27s_Championship "2022 SAFF Women's Championship") at the [Dasharath Rangasala](/wiki/Dasharath_Rangasala "Dasharath Rangasala") in [Kathmandu](/wiki/Kathmandu "Kathmandu"). Pakistan lost 3–0,[[17]](#cite_note-17) which was followed by a 6–0 loss against Nepal in the next match which meant the team could not progress to the semifinals.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In the final group stage fixture, Pakistan recorded its biggest win when it defeated Maldives 7–0, courtesy of four goals by [Nadia Khan](/wiki/Nadia_Khan_(footballer) "Nadia Khan (footballer)"), who became the team's joint record goal scorer.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) It was also the first time a player had scored [three goals](/wiki/Hat-trick "Hat-trick") or more in a match for Pakistan.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In January 2023, the team visited [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") for a [four-nation international friendly tournament](/wiki/2023_SAFF_Women%27s_Friendly_Tournament "2023 SAFF Women's Friendly Tournament").[[22]](#cite_note-22) It won its first game 1–0 against [Comoros](/wiki/Comoros_women%27s_national_football_team "Comoros women's national football team"),[[23]](#cite_note-23) the first time it faced a non-[Asian](/wiki/Asian_Football_Confederation "Asian Football Confederation") opponent,[[24]](#cite_note-24) lost the next fixture 2–1 against [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius_women%27s_national_football_team "Mauritius women's national football team"),[[25]](#cite_note-25) before drawing the final match 1–1 against hosts [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia_women%27s_national_football_team "Saudi Arabia women's national football team").[[26]](#cite_note-26) With four points in three matches, Pakistan finished as runners-up at the tournament,[[27]](#cite_note-27) with captain Maria Khan being declared as the player of the tournament.

In April 2023, Pakistan played its first competitive fixtures when it made its first appearance in the first round of the [2024 AFC Women's Olympic Qualifying Tournament](/wiki/2024_AFC_Women%27s_Olympic_Qualifying_Tournament "2024 AFC Women's Olympic Qualifying Tournament"). The team lost 4–0 against [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines_women%27s_national_football_team "Philippines women's national football team")[[28]](#cite_note-28) and 2–0 against [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong_women%27s_national_football_team "Hong Kong women's national football team"),[[29]](#cite_note-29) but won 1–0 against hosts [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan_women%27s_national_football_team "Tajikistan women's national football team").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The team was to visit [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") in July for two friendly fixtures against the [host team](/wiki/Singapore_women%27s_national_football_team "Singapore women's national football team"). However, their departure was delayed due to visa issues which meant only one match could be played.[[31]](#cite_note-:4-31) Singapore won that match 1–0.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In August, it was confirmed that Pakistan will participate in a six-team tournament to be held in Saudi Arabia in September.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In that tournament, Pakistan drew 0–0 against [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia_women%27s_national_football_team "Malaysia women's national football team") and lost 1–0 against Saudi Arabia in their first two matches.[[34]](#cite_note-34) In their final group match, they won 4–2 on penalties against [Laos](/wiki/Laos_women%27s_national_football_team "Laos women's national football team") after the match ended 1–1. This was the first [penalty shootout](/wiki/Penalty_shootout "Penalty shootout") win for Pakistan.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In 2024, the team competed in the [SAFF Women's Championship](/wiki/2024_SAFF_Women%27s_Championship "2024 SAFF Women's Championship") held in Nepal. It lost 5–2 against India,[[36]](#cite_note-36) and drew 1–1 against eventual champions Bangladesh.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Pakistan also played against Saudi Arabia in a 1–1 draw in friendly match in December of that year.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In 2025, Pakistan competed in its first [AFC Women's Asian Cup qualification](/wiki/AFC_Women%27s_Asian_Cup_qualification "AFC Women's Asian Cup qualification") campaign. As part of Group D, Pakistan played all three of its matches in [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"). After an 8–0 loss against [Chinese Taipei](/wiki/Chinese_Taipei_women%27s_national_football_team "Chinese Taipei women's national football team") in their first match,[[39]](#cite_note-39) Pakistan won its next two matches 2–0 and 2–1 against [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia_women%27s_national_football_team "Indonesia women's national football team") and [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan_women%27s_national_football_team "Kyrgyzstan women's national football team") respectively.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)
